edges.description: AR variants impair receptor expression, ligand binding, DNA binding, or receptor-dependent transcriptional signaling.
Reduced receptor signaling makes androgen-responsive tissues resistant to otherwise age-appropriate androgen exposure.
Complete androgen resistance produces female external genitalia in a 46,XY individual.
Pubic and axillary hair development is reduced because terminal sexual hair growth depends on androgen receptor signaling.
edges.target: Sparse or Absent Terminal Sexual Hair

edges.description: Complete androgen resistance is associated with normal testis development but failure of descent into the scrotum.
Pubertal development is atypical because androgen-responsive tissues do not respond normally despite testicular sex-steroid production.
intermediate_mechanisms: androgen target-tissue resistance during pubertal maturation

edges.description: Complete androgen resistance in a 46,XY individual with retained testes results in infertility.
intermediate_mechanisms: absent functional androgen-dependent male reproductive tract and germline fertility

edges.description: Androgen receptor resistance impairs normal negative feedback and is associated with elevated circulating luteinizing hormone.
intermediate_mechanisms: impaired androgen negative feedback at the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis

edges.description: Testes continue to produce testosterone despite peripheral androgen resistance, producing male-range or increased serum testosterone in the clinical context.
intermediate_mechanisms: preserved testicular androgen production despite androgen target-tissue resistance

edges.description: Preserved testicular androgen production with peripheral aromatization is associated with increased serum estradiol.
intermediate_mechanisms: aromatization of preserved testicular androgen production

edges.description: Feedback disruption in CAIS can include abnormal follicle-stimulating hormone concentration.
Retained testes with preserved Sertoli-cell function are associated with increased antimullerian hormone concentration.
intermediate_mechanisms: preserved Sertoli-cell antimullerian hormone production in retained testes

edges.description: Androgen resistance can alter pubertal skeletal maturation and is associated with tall stature in CAIS.
Complete androgen resistance produces female external genitalia despite a 46,XY karyotype.
edges.target: Female external genitalia in individual with 46,XY karyotype

edges.description: Loss of androgen-responsive terminal hair development can make axillary hair sparse.
edges.source: Sparse or Absent Terminal Sexual Hair

edges.description: More complete loss of androgen-responsive terminal hair development can produce absent axillary hair.
edges.source: Sparse or Absent Terminal Sexual Hair

edges.description: Loss of androgen-responsive terminal hair development can make pubic hair sparse.
edges.source: Sparse or Absent Terminal Sexual Hair

edges.description: More complete loss of androgen-responsive terminal hair development can produce absent pubic hair.
edges.source: Sparse or Absent Terminal Sexual Hair

edges.description: Regression or absence of Mullerian-derived structures produces abnormal internal genital anatomy.
edges.target: Abnormal morphology of female internal genitalia

edges.description: Mullerian regression in CAIS commonly leaves the uterus absent.
Fallopian tubes are Mullerian-derived structures and are absent or hypoplastic when Mullerian development regresses.
edges.target: Aplasia or hypoplasia of the fallopian tube

edges.description: The uterine cervix is Mullerian-derived and is absent or abnormal along with the uterus.
Absence of upper Mullerian-derived structures produces a blind-ending vagina.
Uterine aplasia and absent Mullerian-derived reproductive structures lead to primary amenorrhea at puberty.
Retained undescended testes manifest clinically as bilateral cryptorchidism.
Retained undescended gonadal tissue creates a tumor-risk management issue that informs gonadectomy decisions.
Retained gonadal tissue in CAIS carries a recognized risk of germ cell neoplasia.
Retained testes in CAIS can develop testicular neoplasia, which motivates tumor-risk surveillance and gonadectomy decisions.
